Managers.observed_pattern: Managers exhibit both very high and very low success rates depending on tactic combinations.
effective_combinations: Button or attachment redirection combined with Data Confirmation or Security Update pretexts
Use of authority or scarcity cues in professional contexts
ineffective_combinations: Shop pretext with weak persuasion cues
Link-only redirection without authority or scarcity
Managers.variability_note: Success rates for managers vary widely across countries, pretexts, and redirection methods, indicating strong interaction effects rather than a uniformly high susceptibility.

Employees.observed_pattern: Employees show moderate success rates with noticeable sensitivity to redirection method.
effective_combinations: Button-based redirection paired with Data Confirmation
Attachments when combined with contextual pretexts
ineffective_combinations: Security Update pretexts without authority cues
Employees.variability_note: Employee responses vary substantially by country and persuasion principle usage, suggesting situational rather than demographic dominance.

Students.observed_pattern: Students generally show lower success rates, with a few high-performing outliers.
effective_combinations: Link or attachment redirection combined with Shop pretexts
Use of authority or commitment cues in younger age ranges
ineffective_combinations: Security Update pretexts without strong persuasion signals
Generic messages lacking personalization or urgency
Students.variability_note: Student success rates fluctuate strongly by country and age range, indicating heterogeneity within this population.

Data Confirmation.observed_effect: Appears across many high and moderate success-rate entries for managers and employees.
Data Confirmation.notes: This pretext performs well when paired with button or attachment redirection and reinforced by authority or commitment cues.

Security Update.observed_effect: Produces mixed outcomes, including both high and very low success rates.
Security Update.notes: Effectiveness appears contingent on authority and scarcity usage; weak persuasion leads to poor outcomes.

Shop.observed_effect: Frequently associated with lower success rates, but with notable exceptions.
Shop.notes: Shop pretexts occasionally perform well when combined with strong authority or commitment cues, particularly in specific age groups.

Button.observed_effect: Often associated with higher success rates across populations.
Button.notes: Button-based interactions appear to reduce friction and are frequently paired with successful Data Confirmation or Shop pretexts.

Attachments.notes: Attachments can be very effective or very ineffective depending on pretext and persuasion cues, indicating high risk–reward behavior.

Link.observed_effect: More commonly associated with moderate to low success rates.
Link.notes: Links perform better when supported by authority cues or when aligned with contextual expectations.

Authority.observed_effect: Frequently present in higher success-rate entries for managers and employees.
Authority.notes: Authority cues appear less consistently effective for students unless combined with relevance or commitment.

Scarcity.observed_effect: Appears in both successful and unsuccessful emails.
Scarcity.notes: Scarcity alone does not guarantee success and is more effective when paired with Security Update or Data Confirmation pretexts.

Commitment.observed_effect: Associated with several higher success-rate outcomes across populations.
Commitment.notes: Commitment cues appear most effective when the email implies an existing relationship or prior interaction.

USA.observed_pattern: Displays a wide range of success rates, including many high-performing combinations.
USA.notes: USA entries frequently include Data Confirmation pretexts and button-based redirection.

Netherlands.observed_pattern: Shows moderate success rates with fewer extreme highs.
Netherlands.notes: Security Update and Data Confirmation pretexts dominate, with mixed effectiveness.

Italy.observed_pattern: Includes many lower success-rate entries, with some strong exceptions.
Italy.notes: Shop and Security Update pretexts appear more frequently, with effectiveness depending strongly on persuasion cue usage.

recommendations: Evaluate phishing risk based on combinations of pretext, redirection method, and persuasion cues rather than population alone.
Treat attachment-based emails as high-variance signals requiring contextual evaluation.
Flag button-based Data Confirmation emails targeting managers and employees as higher risk.
Avoid assuming uniform susceptibility within any population; country and age range materially affect outcomes.
